text:
To say "Mario" as if to rhyme with "stereo" is total disgrace among Italians and Italian-Americans. It's also like trying to say the Chinese surname "Wang" as if to rhyme with "fang" or "gang," rather than with "long" or "wrong."

text:
Despite the cheesy commercial, that game is good fun!

I have the NES Mario Bros. game, and it is all about timing and bravery (and maybe even about luck) if you want to score some points in that game.

I also enjoy playing the updated game that Nintendo put onto the GameBoy Advance: Super Mario Advance 3-Yoshi's Island cartridge.  The controls are better, and the graphics are improved...but it is still just as fun as the original game.

You guys have a good day.

Actually, that's backwards.

Mah-rio is right, as is ahn-velope. The reason is because the latter is a French word 'stolen' by English. Same with rendezvous, which is "rahn-day-voo" and not "ren-dez-vowz".

text: Okay, this has driven me insane for years. When I was a kid and these games came out, I lived on Long Island, NY. Everyone there pronounced Mario 'mare-io'. Then, I moved up to New England, and everyone pronounced it 'Mahr-io.' Neither are 'more correct', but I always considered 'mare-io' to be right, because the character was from New York, and that's how we pronounced it there.

author: @PeteGeek

text:
No, the deal wouldn't have happened either way. Kassar was attempting to delay the NES until the 7800 could be released. If Atari did manage to get the NES deal, they probably would have sat on it. (See Sun's exclusive Windows Sparc contract with Microsoft for a similar maneuver.)

Kassar's rage at the Coleco DK demo was most likely a delay tactic that did work. Nintendo was locked out of the market until 1985. Too bad the market crash killed Warner-Atari.
author: @thewiirocks

text:
Actually, Atari ended the deal mainly because of a feud they had with Coleco. When Coleco introduced the ADAM, they used Donkey Kong to showcase it. Problem was, Atari had the home computer license to DK while Coleco had the video game license. Atari accused Nintendo of dealing with Coleco behind its back, and when James Morgan became CEO of Atari, the deal was ultimately canceled because it had been delayed by the whole fiasco.

Had Coleco not used Donkey Kong, the deal would've been completed.
author: @thepthepthep

text: The 2600 didn't "kill video games", it was a number of factors that brought on the video game crash of '83. People just love to use a scapegoat, I guess.
